overview.body: BUILD unit. Module 4 opens by killing the hardest pain point from Module 3: those long lists of distance constants and parallel destination lists for every city. The city graph moves out of code and into two text files, loaded into dictionaries at startup. distances['San Antonio']['Houston'] returns 200. Adding ten more cities means adding ten more lines to a text file, not touching code. Everything else from Lab 3.3 carries forward unchanged: the main-menu loop, save and load, the play-again loop, the D20 travel mechanic, the hazard system, and the trip report file. Same game, smarter data shape.

mentorQuote.quote: Real codebases keep data and code in separate places. If you have to edit a function every time the business changes its mind about a city, the design is wrong. Move the data into a file, load it once at startup, and let the code stay still. That is the whole point of Module 4.

objectives: Add load_city_distances(filename) to function_library.py that parses CSV lines with .strip() and .split(',') into a nested dictionary
Add load_city_info(filename) that parses CSV lines into a dictionary of dictionaries with type and tagline keys
Refactor display_destinations, get_destination, and get_redirect in the library to take (city, distances) instead of parallel lists. Same function names, smarter data shape
Add is_win_city and is_dead_end helpers that read from the city info dictionary

Rebuild lab-4-1.py so the city graph is loaded from text files at startup. Keep the three-loop architecture (main menu, play-again, in-game), keep the D20 mechanic, keep the parallel-list hazards inline for now (they move to a file in Lab 4.2)
overview.keyConceptsText: Two new tools arrive together. .strip().split(',') is the standard CSV-parsing pattern from Chapter 8: .strip() removes the newline at the end of each line, .split(',') breaks the comma-separated values into a list of fields. Dictionaries (Chapter 9) replace parallel lists for any data shape where the keys are meaningful. A nested dictionary is just a dictionary whose values are themselves dictionaries: distances['San Antonio'] is itself a dictionary mapping destinations to miles. The in operator works on dictionaries too. 'Houston' in distances checks if Houston is a key. This is how the loader builds the nested structure: it checks if city1 not in distances: before creating the inner dictionary.
overview.textbookReference: Chapter 8 covers the string methods. Programs 8–1 through 8–14 demonstrate .strip(), .split(), and string traversal. Chapter 9 covers dictionaries. Programs 9–1 through 9–7 cover dictionary creation, access, the in operator with dictionaries, and looping through keys.
programRequirements: Top-of-file comment block on both files: Name, abc123, Section, Description
Load both data files at startup in main() using the new library functions
Three-loop architecture preserved: main menu, play-again, in-game (carried forward from Lab 3.3)
D20 mechanic preserved: each turn the player rolls a d20 and moves roll * MILES_MULTIPLIER miles
Inline parallel-list hazards still live in lab-4-1.py for this lab; hazards.txt arrives in Lab 4.2
Trip report saved to trip_report.txt at the end of every completed game

namedConstants.name: DISTANCES_FILE
namedConstants.description: Name of the city distances text file

namedConstants.name: INFO_FILE
namedConstants.description: Name of the city info text file

namedConstants.name: REPORT_FILE
namedConstants.description: Name of the trip report output file

namedConstants.name: MILES_MULTIPLIER
namedConstants.description: Multiplier applied to each D20 roll: roll * 10 miles per turn

namedConstants.name: HAZARD_CHANCE
namedConstants.description: Percent chance per turn of a hazard event (30)

overview.filenameNote: Submit lab-4-1.py and function_library.py this unit. CodeGrade provides the data files (city_distances.txt, city_info.txt) as fixtures, so you do not upload them. Test locally with the copies you download from this page.

dataFiles.format: Your shared function library, carried over from Module 3 and extended this unit

rows.task: Add load_city_info using the same parsing pattern
Refactor display_destinations / get_destination / get_redirect to dict shape; add is_win_city and is_dead_end
Start lab-4-1.py, load data files in main(), keep the main-menu loop
In-game loop with coin-flip Destination Gate, D20 movement, dead-end handling
Trip report on screen plus trip_report.txt write, session accumulators
Full play-through, then upload both files to CodeGrade

beforeYouBegin.intro: Here is what your finished program looks like. [NEW] tags show what gets added this unit. [REPLACED] tags show what changes shape but keeps the same function name. [KEEP] tags show what carries forward from Lab 3.3 untouched.
beforeYouBegin.architecture:
# ---- IN function_library.py ----
flip_coin(), roll_d20(), check_percentage()                 [KEEP from 2.2]
get_valid_int(), get_yes_no()                               [KEEP from 2.3]
save_game(...), load_game()                                 [KEEP from 3.2]
display_numbered_list(items, title)                         [KEEP from 3.2]
get_random_hazard(names, heads_msgs, tails_msgs)            [KEEP from 3.3]

load_city_distances(filename) -> nested dict                [NEW-CP1]
load_city_info(filename) -> dict of dicts                   [NEW-CP2]
display_destinations(city, distances)                       [REPLACED-CP3] was parallel lists
get_destination(city, distances, choice)                    [REPLACED-CP3] was parallel lists
get_redirect(city, distances, avoid)                        [REPLACED-CP3] was parallel lists
is_win_city(city, city_info)                                [NEW-CP3]
is_dead_end(city, city_info)                                [NEW-CP3]

# ---- IN lab-4-1.py ----

# ---- CONSTANTS ----
DISTANCES_FILE = 'city_distances.txt'                       [NEW-CP4]
INFO_FILE      = 'city_info.txt'                            [NEW-CP4]
REPORT_FILE    = 'trip_report.txt'                          [KEEP from 3.3]
MILES_MULTIPLIER = 10                                       [KEEP from 3.3]
HAZARD_CHANCE    = 30                                       [KEEP from 3.3]

# ---- HAZARD DATA (still inline; moves to file in Lab 4.2) ----
HAZARD_NAMES, HAZARD_HEADS_MSGS, HAZARD_TAILS_MSGS          [KEEP from 3.3]

# ---- LAB-LOCAL HELPER ----
save_trip_report(filename, ...)                             [KEEP from 3.3]

# ---- main() ----
Load data files: distances = load_city_distances(...)       [NEW-CP4]
                 city_info = load_city_info(...)            [NEW-CP4]

MAIN MENU LOOP                                              [KEEP from 3.3]
  1. New Game  2. Load Saved Game  3. Quit
  │
  │   PLAY-AGAIN LOOP                                       [KEEP from 3.3]
  │   │
  │   │   IN-GAME LOOP                                       [KEEP from 3.3]
  │   │   │─ Show current city + tagline from city_info     [NEW-CP5]
  │   │   │─ display_destinations(current_city, distances)  [REPLACED-CP5]
  │   │   │─ Coin-flip Destination Gate                     [KEEP from 3.3]
  │   │   │─ D20 travel loop with hazard check              [KEEP from 3.3]
  │   │   │─ is_win_city / is_dead_end branching            [NEW-CP5]
  │   │   │─ Continue / Save & quit / Quit                  [KEEP from 3.3]
  │   │
  │   │─ Trip report on screen + trip_report.txt              [KEEP-CP6 from 3.3]
  │   │─ Session accumulators update                          [KEEP-CP6 from 3.3]
  │   │─ Play again? (y/n)                                    [KEEP from 3.3]
  │
  │─ Session statistics block                                  [KEEP from 3.3]
  │─ Back to main menu

content.body: A new function in function_library.py named load_city_distances(filename). It opens the named text file, reads it one line at a time, parses each line into a city pair plus a distance, and builds a nested dictionary where the outer key is the starting city, the inner key is the destination, and the inner value is the distance in miles.

Lab 3.3 declared one constant per route at the top of lab-3-3.py: SAT_TO_CORPUS = 150, SAT_TO_HOUSTON = 200, and so on. That works for three cities and ten edges. It does not work for ten cities and twenty-four edges, which is what Module 4 hands you. The fix is to keep the data in a text file and write one function that reads it. Adding a city later means adding a line to the text file, not editing code. Same data, different storage shape, dramatically less work downstream.

content.quote: If you had to add twenty more cities to this game next unit, how long would it take? If the answer is 'just add twenty lines to a text file', the design is right. If the answer is 'edit the code in five places', the design is wrong. Pick the data shape that makes the next change small.

content.title: How the Road Network Is Modeled
content.body: The 24 routes in city_distances.txt are the cities you can drive between on a Texas interstate. San Antonio reaches Houston (I-10), Austin (I-35), Laredo (I-35), El Paso (I-10), and Corpus Christi (I-37). Houston reaches Galveston (I-45) and Dallas (I-45). Austin reaches Dallas and Fort Worth via the I-35E and I-35W split. Dallas and Fort Worth connect via I-30; Dallas reaches Texarkana via I-30; Fort Worth reaches El Paso via I-20 and I-10. There is no interstate between Austin and Houston (US-290 is a state highway), between Laredo and El Paso, or between Corpus Christi and Laredo, so those routes do not appear in the data. To get between two cities that aren't connected directly, the player has to drive through an intermediate city — usually San Antonio. If a route feels missing, that's the constraint at work, not a bug in the data file. Adding a city means committing to the same standard: if there's a clean interstate between the two cities, the edge belongs in the file; otherwise it does not.

items: Open function_library.py. At the bottom, add a new section header comment block titled CITY DATA LOADERS.
Define load_city_distances(filename) with a full docstring describing what it returns.
Inside the function, create an empty dictionary called distances.
Open the file with infile = open(filename, 'r').
Loop through the file with for line in infile:. For each line, call parts = line.strip().split(',') to split it into a list of three strings.
Assign city1 = parts[0], city2 = parts[1], and miles = int(parts[2]). Only the third part needs int() — the first two are city names.
If city1 is not yet a key in distances, set distances[city1] = {}. Then set distances[city1][city2] = miles.
After the loop, close the file with infile.close() and return distances.

references.description: You used .split('/') to break a date string into parts. Same idea here, but split on ',' instead. The result is a list of strings you can index by position.
You created a dictionary mapping names to phone numbers. Same idea, but the outer dictionary's values are themselves dictionaries: distances[city1][city2] = miles.

content.body: Add a temporary test block at the bottom of function_library.py under the if __name__ == '__main__': guard. Run the library directly with python3 function_library.py and confirm the dictionary loads correctly. Delete the test block before you submit.

content.code:
# DEBUG - DELETE BEFORE CHECKPOINT 2
distances = load_city_distances('city_distances.txt')
print('Cities with outgoing routes:', list(distances.keys()))
print('From San Antonio:', distances['San Antonio'])
print('SA to Houston:', distances['San Antonio']['Houston'])

rows: Forgetting .strip()
Each line ends with a newline character. Without .strip(), your last 'destination' key has a hidden \n on the end, and every lookup mysteriously fails.
Casting parts[0] or parts[1] to int
Only the distance is a number. City names are strings. Doing int(parts[0]) throws ValueError the moment you hit a non-numeric city name.
Skipping the if city1 not in distances: guard
Without it, the very first time you see a city you also try to assign distances[city1][city2], which fails because distances[city1] does not exist yet.
Trying to use with open(...) as ...
with statements are not in scope for this lab. Use the plain open()/close() pattern from Chapter 6.

items: function_library.py contains a new function load_city_distances(filename) with a full docstring
Running the library directly prints all ten cities and the expected San Antonio routes
The DEBUG block is removed before moving on to CP2

content.body: A second loader function, load_city_info(filename), that parses city_info.txt into a dictionary of dictionaries: each city name maps to an inner dictionary with two keys, 'type' (one of START, HUB, GATEWAY, CORRIDOR, WRONG_WAY, DEAD_END, WIN) and 'tagline' (the city's short slogan).

City info is the metadata that drives game logic. 'WIN' cities end the trip with a beach celebration. 'DEAD_END' and 'WRONG_WAY' cities prompt the player to return to San Antonio. 'GATEWAY', 'HUB', 'CORRIDOR' are all just intermediate cities for now. Bundling all of this into one dictionary keyed by city name means the lab file does not need branching logic per city; it just asks the info dictionary what to do.

content.quote: Notice that the loader pattern is identical to the one you wrote ten minutes ago. Same .strip().split(','). Same per-line dictionary assignment. Different data shape on the output side, same parsing pattern on the input side. That is what good design feels like. The boring part is reusable.

items: Below load_city_distances in function_library.py, define load_city_info(filename) with a full docstring.
Create an empty dictionary called info.
Open the file with open(filename, 'r') and loop with for line in infile:.
Inside the loop, call parts = line.strip().split(',') and assign city = parts[0], city_type = parts[1], tagline = parts[2].
Assign info[city] = {'type': city_type, 'tagline': tagline} — one statement creates the inner dictionary inline.
Close the file and return info.

references.description: Same dictionary-creation pattern. The only difference: the value associated with each key is itself a dictionary, not a scalar. That is what makes this a 'dictionary of dictionaries.'

content.body: Add a DEBUG block under the if __name__ == '__main__': guard in function_library.py that loads city_info.txt and prints three lookups. Delete it before moving on.

content.code:
# DEBUG - DELETE BEFORE CHECKPOINT 3
info = load_city_info('city_info.txt')
print('San Antonio type:', info['San Antonio']['type'])
print('Corpus Christi tagline:', info['Corpus Christi']['tagline'])
print('Laredo type:', info['Laredo']['type'])

content.output:
San Antonio type: START
Corpus Christi tagline: Sparkling City by the Sea
Laredo type: DEAD_END

rows: Putting the type and tagline at the same level as the city
info[city] = type overwrites with a string, not a dictionary. You need info[city] = {'type': city_type, 'tagline': tagline} — note the curly braces inside.
Quoting the type values as 'win'
The file uses uppercase WIN. String comparisons are case-sensitive. Match exactly or use .upper() when you compare in CP3 (preview: you will not need .upper() until Lab 4.2).
Splitting on the wrong delimiter
The file is comma-separated, not tab- or space-separated. .split(',') exactly.

items: load_city_info defined in the same CITY DATA LOADERS section as load_city_distances
DEBUG print confirms all three lookups produce the expected values
DEBUG block removed before moving on

content.body: The library's destination helpers from Lab 3.2 take parallel lists (destinations, distances, notes). Now they take (city, distances) — the city name plus the loaded nested dictionary. Same function names, same return shape, smarter data. You will also add two new type-check helpers, is_win_city and is_dead_end, that read from the city info dictionary.

Keeping the function names stable across a data-shape refactor is a real-world discipline. The rest of the lab file calls display_destinations(...) the same way it always has. What changes is what you pass in. That is one of the few ways a working program can absorb a new data shape without rewriting everything that uses it. The new is_win_city and is_dead_end helpers move the game's branching logic out of the lab file and into named, testable functions.

content.quote: When you refactor a function, keep the public name and shape stable if you can. The callers do not care what the function does inside — they care what they pass in and what they get back. display_destinations still prints a numbered menu and returns a count. The shape that gets passed in is different now. The shape that comes out is the same. That is the kind of change that does not break the rest of your code.

items: In function_library.py, find the DESTINATION HELPERS section from Lab 3.2.

Rewrite display_destinations. New signature: display_destinations(city, distances). Inside, loop over distances[city] with a 1-based counter, printing {n}. {dest} - {miles} miles. Return the number of destinations shown.

Rewrite get_destination. New signature: get_destination(city, distances, choice). Build a list of destination names from list(distances[city].keys()), index it with choice - 1, and return a tuple (dest, distances[city][dest]).

Rewrite get_redirect. New signature: get_redirect(city, distances, avoid). Build the options list the same way, remove avoid if present, pick a random remaining option, and return (dest, distances[city][dest]).
Add a new section header comment CITY-INFO TYPE CHECKS.
Define is_win_city(city, city_info): return True if city_info[city]['type'] == 'WIN'.
Define is_dead_end(city, city_info): return True if the city's type is 'DEAD_END' or 'WRONG_WAY'. Both count as dead ends for gameplay purposes.

references.description: You looped through a dictionary with for key in dict:. Same pattern in display_destinations: iterate over the inner dictionary distances[city].
You used in to check for a key. is_win_city and is_dead_end use the same idea: ask the dictionary whether a city's type matches the expected value.

content.body: Add a DEBUG block in function_library.py that loads both files and exercises all five rewrites. Delete the block before moving on.

content.code:
# DEBUG - DELETE BEFORE CHECKPOINT 4
distances = load_city_distances('city_distances.txt')
info = load_city_info('city_info.txt')

n = display_destinations('San Antonio', distances)
print(f'Total destinations from SA: {n}')

dest, miles = get_destination('San Antonio', distances, 2)
print(f'Choice 2 from SA -> {dest} ({miles} miles)')

print('Corpus Christi is a WIN:', is_win_city('Corpus Christi', info))
print('Laredo is a dead end:', is_dead_end('Laredo', info))
print('El Paso is a dead end:', is_dead_end('El Paso', info))
print('San Antonio is a dead end:', is_dead_end('San Antonio', info))

content.output:
From San Antonio, you can travel to:
  1. Corpus Christi - 150 miles
  2. Houston - 200 miles
  3. Austin - 80 miles
  4. Laredo - 160 miles
  5. El Paso - 550 miles
Total destinations from SA: 5
Choice 2 from SA -> Houston (200 miles)
Corpus Christi is a WIN: True
Laredo is a dead end: True
El Paso is a dead end: True
San Antonio is a dead end: False

rows: Old-style function call from the lab file
display_destinations(SA_DESTINATIONS, SA_DISTANCES, SA_NOTES) is the Lab 3.2 signature. The new signature is display_destinations(current_city, distances). Update every call site in CP5.
Forgetting to return the count
display_destinations still returns the number of destinations shown. The lab uses that count in the get_valid_int(f'Choice (1-{n}): ', 1, n) call.
Treating WRONG_WAY as different from DEAD_END
Gameplay-wise they behave identically: prompt the player to return to San Antonio. is_dead_end returns True for both.
Removing 'avoid' from the dictionary itself
get_redirect builds a list of options and removes avoid from that list. The original dictionary stays untouched. Otherwise you destroy the loaded game state.

items: Three rewritten helpers (display_destinations, get_destination, get_redirect) with new (city, distances) signatures
Two new type-check helpers (is_win_city, is_dead_end) in a new CITY-INFO TYPE CHECKS section
All five functions have docstrings
DEBUG block removed

content.body: Start lab-4-1.py. The file imports function_library, declares the five module-level constants, declares the inline hazard parallel lists (carried forward from Lab 3.3), defines save_trip_report as a lab-local helper (also carried forward), and opens main() with the file loads and the main-menu loop from Lab 3.3.

The main-menu loop is the outermost structure that lets the player choose New Game, Load Saved Game, or Quit. It was working in Lab 3.3. The only thing that changes here is that the city graph data is loaded from text files before the main-menu loop starts. After that, the menu logic, save/load logic, and welcome banner all stay the same.

items: Create lab-4-1.py with a top-of-file comment block (Name, abc123, Section, Description) and a module docstring.
import function_library at the top.
Declare module-level constants: DISTANCES_FILE = 'city_distances.txt', INFO_FILE = 'city_info.txt', REPORT_FILE = 'trip_report.txt', MILES_MULTIPLIER = 10, HAZARD_CHANCE = 30.
Copy the three parallel hazard lists (HAZARD_NAMES, HAZARD_HEADS_MSGS, HAZARD_TAILS_MSGS) from Lab 3.3.
Copy save_trip_report from Lab 3.3 into the lab file unchanged.

Define main(). The first thing it does is print the welcome banner and load the data files: distances = function_library.load_city_distances(DISTANCES_FILE) and city_info = function_library.load_city_info(INFO_FILE). Print the load counts so the player sees the game started cleanly.
Add the main-menu loop from Lab 3.3 unchanged: a while in_program: loop offering New Game (1), Load Saved Game (2), or Quit (3). Handle Quit and the load-from-file branch exactly like 3.3.
Add the if __name__ == '__main__': guard at the bottom calling main().
Run the program. Confirm the welcome banner shows the loaded counts. Choose 3 to quit immediately and verify the program exits cleanly.

references.description: Same idea: load a dictionary at program startup, then use it through the rest of the program. The loading happens once; everything downstream reads the loaded dictionary.

content.body: Run the program. Expected behavior at this checkpoint: welcome banner, load counts print (10 cities, 10 info records), main menu shows, choosing 3 exits cleanly. Choosing 1 or 2 will not work yet because the play-again loop is built in CP5.

content.output:
==================================================
DEEP IN THE HEART: A LONE STAR JOURNEY
==================================================

Loading city data...
Loaded 10 cities with travel routes.
Loaded 10 city info records.

MAIN MENU
--------------------
1. New Game
2. Load Saved Game
3. Quit

Choice (1-3): 3

Thanks for playing!

rows: Loading inside the main-menu loop
Load the data files ONCE, before the loop. Loading inside the loop re-reads the files every iteration.
Hardcoding file names
Use the DISTANCES_FILE and INFO_FILE constants. CodeGrade looks for those exact names.
Forgetting if __name__ == '__main__':
Without it, main() runs at import time, which breaks CodeGrade's test harness.

items: lab-4-1.py with top-of-file comment, docstring, imports, constants, hazard lists, save_trip_report, main()
Running the program shows the welcome banner, loads 10 cities and 10 info records, displays the main menu
Choosing 3 exits cleanly with a thank-you message

checkpoints.title: Coin Gate + D20 Travel + Dead Ends

content.body: Drop the play-again loop and the in-game loop from Lab 3.3 into main(). Update every call site to use the new dict-shape helpers: display_destinations(current_city, distances), get_destination(current_city, distances, choice), and get_redirect(current_city, distances, wanted). Replace the hardcoded if current_city in ('Corpus Christi', 'Galveston') and elif current_city == 'Austin' branches with calls to is_win_city and is_dead_end.

The in-game loop is identical in structure to Lab 3.3: pick a destination, flip the coin gate, travel by D20, check for hazards, check arrival type, decide next action. What changes is that every reference to a hardcoded city name moves out into the city_info dictionary lookup. A new city in the data file does not need any code changes here. That is the win.

items: Inside the main-menu loop's New Game branch, initialize: current_city = 'San Antonio', games_played = 0, session_miles = 0, session_hazards = 0.

Open the play-again loop with play_again = True; while play_again:. Inside, reinitialize per-game state (total_miles, total_turns, total_hazards, total_escaped, visited_cities, hazards_list) unless this iteration is resuming a save.
Open the in-game loop with playing = True; while playing:. Inside, fetch the current city's info, print it with its tagline, then call function_library.display_destinations(current_city, distances).

Use function_library.get_valid_int to read the player's menu choice. Then flip the Destination Gate coin: on HEADS call get_destination, on TAILS call get_destination first to capture the wanted destination, then call get_redirect to pick a different destination instead.

Drop in the D20 travel loop from Lab 3.3 unchanged. Each turn: roll = function_library.roll_d20(), miles_this_turn = roll * MILES_MULTIPLIER, decrement miles_remaining, add to total_miles, and check for hazards via function_library.check_percentage(HAZARD_CHANCE).

After arrival, update current_city, append to visited_cities, and check what kind of city you arrived at: if is_win_city(current_city, city_info) you reached the beach (set reached_beach = True, exit the in-game loop). If is_dead_end(current_city, city_info), print the dead-end message and use function_library.get_yes_no to ask 'Return to San Antonio?'.
Otherwise print the 1/2/3 Continue / Save & quit / Quit without saving menu and dispatch accordingly. Save & quit calls function_library.save_game(...) with the 11-tuple.

references.description: 'Corpus Christi' in distances works the same way as your earlier in checks. But you do not need to write the city names by hand — is_win_city and is_dead_end wrap the comparison.

content.body: Run a full happy-path play-through: New Game, enter your name, pick Corpus Christi (choice 1), let the coin flip however it lands, and ride the D20 mechanic to the beach. Then play a dead-end path: pick Laredo (choice 4), say no to the return-home prompt, and confirm the game ends with a 'Dead End' status.

rows: Skipping the redirect path on TAILS
When the gate is TAILS, you still need to know what the player wanted. Call get_destination first to capture the wanted dest, then call get_redirect to pick something else.
Forgetting to append to visited_cities after a return-to-SA
If the player is at Laredo and chooses to return, current_city = 'San Antonio' and visited_cities.append('San Antonio'). Otherwise the route looks wrong.
Hardcoding 'Corpus Christi' or 'Galveston' as win cities
Use is_win_city(current_city, city_info) instead. Same outcome, future-proof.
Not resetting per-game state inside the play-again loop
After 'Play again? y', you need a fresh total_miles, visited_cities, etc. Only the session-level counters carry across games.

items: Working three-loop architecture (main menu, play-again, in-game) reading from the loaded dictionaries
Coin-flip Destination Gate working with the new dict-shape helpers
D20 travel loop working with hazards
Dead-end (Laredo, Texarkana, El Paso) and WIN (Corpus Christi, Galveston) handling via is_dead_end and is_win_city
Save & quit writes savegame.txt; loading from that save resumes correctly

content.body: Drop the Lab 3.3 final trip report block in below the in-game loop. Sort the hazards alphabetically with .copy().sort(). Build the unique-cities list using the in operator (sets arrive in Lab 4.3). Call the lab-local save_trip_report to write trip_report.txt. Update the session accumulators. Then ask 'Play again?' to close the play-again loop. After the loop, print the session statistics block from 3.3 unchanged.

The trip report is the player's souvenir. It carries forward from Lab 3.3 because the game's identity, the shape of the data players hold on to, has not changed this unit. Module 4 is about how the game's code reads its data, not what the player experiences. The session statistics show that the play-again loop is doing its job: totals accumulate across multiple games in a single sitting.

content.quote: Test the trip report end-to-end. Run a quick game to the beach, run a dead-end game, run a save-and-quit, run a quit-without-saving. Each one should produce a different status string in the report. If they all say 'BEACH WINNER!' regardless of outcome, the status logic broke and you'll find it in the conditional chain right after the in-game loop. Status strings are the easiest part of this CP to test and the easiest part to get wrong.

items: Immediately after the in-game loop, check the four flags from CP5 (reached_beach, hit_dead_end, saved_and_quit, quit_without_saving) and assign a status string accordingly. The four allowed values are 'BEACH WINNER!', 'Dead End', 'Quit without saving', and 'Trip Incomplete'.
If the player saved and quit, do not write the trip report; instead break out of the play-again loop with play_again = False.
Otherwise, build sorted_hazards = hazards_list.copy() then sorted_hazards.sort().
Build unique_cities by iterating visited_cities and appending each city that is not already in unique_cities. This is the same pattern as Lab 3.3 — sets arrive in 4.3.

Print the trip report block from Lab 3.3: title banner, driver, final city, status, route via display_numbered_list, unique count, hazards (sorted) via display_numbered_list or a 'None' fallback, and the STATISTICS block (miles, turns, hazards faced/escaped, escape rate).
Call save_trip_report(REPORT_FILE, ...) to write the same content to disk.
Update the session accumulators: games_played += 1, session_miles += total_miles, session_hazards += total_hazards.
Ask play_again = function_library.get_yes_no('Play again? (y/n): '). The play-again loop continues while this stays True.
After the play-again loop exits, print the SESSION STATISTICS banner from 3.3 if the player did not save-and-quit.

references.description: The in operator on a list, the same one you used in Lab 3.3 to build unique-city lists. Carries forward unchanged here. Sets arrive in Lab 4.3 and clean this pattern up.
.sort() sorts a list in place. Pair with .copy() to keep the original list's order intact (you still want visited_cities in chronological order for the route display).

content.body: Run all four outcome paths: (1) beach via Corpus Christi, (2) dead-end at Laredo by saying no to the return-home prompt, (3) save and quit at a non-beach city, (4) quit without saving at a non-beach city. Each should produce the correct status. Then play two consecutive games in one session (beach + dead-end) and verify the SESSION STATISTICS at the end show Games Played: 2.

rows: Calling hazards_list.sort() directly
That mutates the original. Use sorted_hazards = hazards_list.copy() first, then sort the copy. The trip report wants sorted, the save format wants chronological.
Updating session accumulators inside the in-game loop
Wait until after the in-game loop exits. Otherwise totals get added to mid-game and the accounting drifts.
Writing the trip report on save-and-quit
On save-and-quit, the player wants to come back later. Skip the report so they do not get a partial souvenir.
Forgetting to include both screen and file output
The trip report runs twice: once to the screen (print statements) and once to the file (save_trip_report). Same content, two destinations.

items: Four distinct status strings produced by the four outcome paths
trip_report.txt written every completed game (not on save-and-quit)
Session accumulators correctly summing across multiple plays
Play-again loop transitions cleanly back to the main menu when the player says no

beforeYouSubmit: function_library.py grew by seven entries this unit: two loaders (load_city_distances, load_city_info), three rewritten helpers (display_destinations, get_destination, get_redirect), and two new type checks (is_win_city, is_dead_end)
lab-4-1.py defines five constants at module level: DISTANCES_FILE, INFO_FILE, REPORT_FILE, MILES_MULTIPLIER, HAZARD_CHANCE
main() loads both data files at startup using the new library functions; the loaded distances and city_info dictionaries flow through the rest of the game
Three-loop architecture from Lab 3.3 is preserved: main menu, play-again, in-game
D20 travel mechanic from Lab 3.3 is preserved: each turn rolls a d20 and moves roll * MILES_MULTIPLIER miles
save_trip_report still lives in lab-4-1.py as a lab-local helper and writes trip_report.txt at the end of every completed game
Top-of-file comment block present on both files: Name, abc123, Section, Description
finalChecklist.scopeReminder: Module 4.1 introduces file parsing (.strip(), .split()) and dictionaries (creation, access, nested, .keys(), 'in' operator). Sets and the .upper() input-cleaning relief are held back for Labs 4.2 and 4.3.

permittedTechniques.specific: Chapter 8: .strip(), .split(',') for file parsing. Chapter 9: dictionary creation with {}, dictionary access with dict[key], nested dictionaries, 'in' with dictionaries, .keys(), looping through dictionaries. Everything from Modules 1–3 carries forward.
permittedTechniques.notPermitted: .upper() and .lower() on user input (saved for Lab 4.2). Sets (saved for Lab 4.3). Trivia gate (saved for Lab 4.3). Dictionary comprehensions (not covered this semester).

warnings: Do not paste the data files into the top of lab-4-1.py as Python literals. The whole point of this unit is that the data lives in text files, not in code.
Do not delete the inline hazard parallel lists from lab-4-1.py. They stay this unit; hazards.txt arrives in Lab 4.2.
finalChecklist.saveYourCode: Save a copy of lab-4-1.py as lab-4-1-backup.py before you start Lab 4.2. Lab 4.2 starts by copying 4.1 forward, so you want a clean baseline to fall back to if the 4.2 changes break something.

ifSomethingBreaks.symptom: KeyError: 'San Antonio' on the first menu display
ifSomethingBreaks.redirect: Check CP1 (Load City Distances) — the loader is not inserting 'San Antonio' as a top-level key. Verify the if city1 not in distances: guard.
Check CP4 (Lab Skeleton). The data files must be in the same folder as lab-4-1.py when you run it locally. CodeGrade provides them as fixtures.

ifSomethingBreaks.symptom: Menu shows distances but the wrong destinations (Houston instead of Corpus Christi, etc.)
ifSomethingBreaks.redirect: Check CP3 (Graph Helpers). Verify display_destinations iterates distances[city] in order and prints both the destination key and the value.

ifSomethingBreaks.symptom: Dead-end prompt fires at the wrong cities (or never fires)
ifSomethingBreaks.redirect: Check CP3 (is_dead_end). Both 'DEAD_END' and 'WRONG_WAY' count as dead ends. Compare exact uppercase strings.

ifSomethingBreaks.symptom: Trip report does not appear at the end of the game
ifSomethingBreaks.redirect: Check CP6. The trip report runs after the in-game loop exits naturally. If the player saves and quits, the report is intentionally skipped (that's the save path, not the report path).

ifSomethingBreaks.symptom: ValueError: invalid literal for int() when loading distances
ifSomethingBreaks.redirect: Check CP1. After parts = line.strip().split(','), only parts[2] gets cast to int. parts[0] and parts[1] stay as strings.

ifYouGetStuck: Re-read Programs 8–10 (.split('/')) and 9–1 (basic dictionaries) before you start. The CSV parsing pattern (line.strip().split(',')) is the lab's most common bug source
Print the loaded dictionaries during development. After distances = load_city_distances(...), add print(distances) to confirm the shape before you use it. Delete the print before submitting
If display_destinations shows nothing, you probably passed the wrong arguments. The new signature is (city, distances) — the city is a string, the distances is the whole nested dictionary

Save / load was working in Lab 3.3 and should still work here unchanged. If it stops working, you probably touched the library save/load functions accidentally. Roll those back to the Module 3 version
Post questions in #labs on Discord. Include your error message and the line number, not a screenshot of the whole file
